cheatSheet:
# JavaScript & TypeScript — Quick Reference

## var / let / const
- `var`: function-scoped, hoisted (initialized to undefined), can re-declare
- `let`: block-scoped, hoisted but not initialized (TDZ), no re-declare
- `const`: block-scoped, must initialize, no reassignment (object internals mutable)

## Hoisting
- Function declarations hoisted fully (callable before declaration)
- `var` hoisted as `undefined`; `let`/`const` in Temporal Dead Zone until line reached

## Closures
- A function that retains access to its lexical scope even when executed outside it
- Classic use: factory functions, memoization, module pattern, event handlers

## Prototype Chain
- Every object has `[[Prototype]]`; property lookup walks the chain until null
- `Object.create(proto)` sets prototype explicitly
- ES6 `class` is syntactic sugar over prototype-based inheritance

## Event Loop
- Call stack → Web APIs → Task Queue (macrotasks) → Microtask Queue
- Microtasks (Promises, queueMicrotask) drain completely before next macrotask
- `setTimeout(fn, 0)` is a macrotask; `Promise.resolve().then(fn)` is a microtask

## Promises & async/await
```js
// Promise chaining
fetch(url).then(r => r.json()).then(data => ...).catch(err => ...);
// async/await
async function load() {
  try { const data = await fetch(url).then(r => r.json()); }
  catch (e) { console.error(e); }
}
```

## TypeScript Key Types
```ts
type ID = string | number;           // union
type Point = { x: number; y: number }; // object type
interface User { id: ID; name: string; email?: string; }
function identity<T>(arg: T): T { return arg; } // generic
enum Direction { Up, Down, Left, Right } // numeric enum
type Partial<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] }; // utility type
```

## Destructuring & Spread
```js
const { a, b = 10 } = obj;          // object destructuring with default
const [first, ...rest] = arr;       // array destructuring with rest
const merged = { ...obj1, ...obj2 }; // spread (last key wins)
```

## Map / Set
```js
const map = new Map(); map.set('key', val); map.get('key');
const set = new Set([1,2,3]); set.has(2); // O(1) lookup
```

## Error Handling
```js
try { riskyOp(); }
catch (e) { if (e instanceof TypeError) { /* handle */ } }
finally { cleanup(); }
```

## Modules
```js
// ESM
export const PI = 3.14;  export default function fn() {}
import fn, { PI } from './mod.js';
// CommonJS
module.exports = { fn }; const { fn } = require('./mod');
```

resources.title: You Don't Know JS Yet (book series)
resources.author: Kyle Simpson
resources.justification: The deepest free resource on JS internals — scope, closures, prototypes, async. Required reading for senior JS interviews.

resources.title: TypeScript Handbook (Official)
resources.author: Microsoft
resources.justification: The authoritative TypeScript reference. Covers types, generics, utility types, and declaration files with precise language.

resources.title: JavaScript.info
resources.author: Ilya Kantor
resources.justification: Best structured tutorial on the web for JS fundamentals through advanced async patterns. Interactive tasks after each chapter.

resources.title: Effective TypeScript
resources.author: Dan Vanderkam
resources.justification: 62 specific, actionable ways to improve TypeScript usage — ideal for candidates who know the basics and want depth.

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Understands var/let/const, basic functions, and DOM manipulation. Can write simple scripts.
observableSkills: Explain the difference between var, let, and const
Write a function using arrow syntax and regular syntax
Handle a button click with addEventListener

milestoneProject.title: Todo List
milestoneProject.description: Build a todo list using vanilla JS with add, complete, and delete functionality. Use localStorage to persist items.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Understands closures, prototypes, Promises, and basic async patterns. Can write modular code.
observableSkills: Explain closure and give a practical use case
Describe the prototype chain and how inheritance works

milestoneProject.title: GitHub User Search
milestoneProject.description: Fetch GitHub user data and repositories using the GitHub API. Handle loading, error, and empty states. Use async/await throughout.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Deep understanding of the event loop, TypeScript generics, and design patterns. Writes type-safe, testable code.
observableSkills: Explain the event loop, microtask queue, and macrotask queue
Write generic TypeScript functions and utility types
Implement debounce and throttle from scratch

milestoneProject.title: Type-Safe Data Fetcher
milestoneProject.description: Build a reusable typed fetch wrapper in TypeScript with generics, retry logic, and request cancellation using AbortController.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Architects performant JS applications. Understands memory management, web workers, and advanced TypeScript patterns.
observableSkills: Identify and fix memory leaks using Chrome DevTools
Use Web Workers for off-main-thread computation
Design a plugin system using Proxy and Symbol

milestoneProject.title: Concurrent Image Processor
milestoneProject.description: Build an image processing pipeline using Web Workers. Main thread UI remains responsive while workers process images. Include a task queue with configurable concurrency.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Contributes to TC39, writes compilers/bundlers, designs type systems, and optimizes for V8 engine internals.
observableSkills: Explain V8 hidden classes, inline caches, and deoptimization
Write a TypeScript transformer or custom ESLint rule

milestoneProject.title: Mini Bundler
milestoneProject.description: Build a minimal JavaScript bundler that resolves ESM imports, builds a dependency graph, and outputs a single bundle file. Support tree shaking for unused exports.

plan.overview: Master JavaScript and TypeScript for software engineering interviews, covering language internals, async patterns, type safety, and performance.
plan.skippedTopics: Node.js-specific APIs, build tool deep dives, and framework-specific patterns are covered in separate topics.

sessions.title: JS Fundamentals — var/let/const, Hoisting, Closures, Scope Chain
sessions.paretoJustification: Hoisting, closures, and scope are asked in nearly every JS interview. Understanding them deeply unlocks answers to dozens of follow-up questions.
objectives: Explain the difference between var, let, and const with respect to scope and hoisting
Describe the Temporal Dead Zone and why it exists
Trace the scope chain for a given piece of code
Implement a closure-based pattern such as a counter, memoizer, or module

activities.description: Write a makeAdder(x) function that returns a function adding x to its argument. Then write makeMultiplier. Compose them: const addThenDouble = compose(makeMultiplier(2), makeAdder(5)). Test with several inputs.

Fix the classic var loop bug: create an array of 5 functions, each returning its index. First use var (observe the bug), then fix with let, then fix with an IIFE. Print results for all three approaches.

reviewQuestions: What is the Temporal Dead Zone and which declarations does it apply to?:::The TDZ is the period between the start of a block scope and the line where a let or const variable is declared. During this period, the variable exists in the scope (it has been hoisted) but has not been initialized, so accessing it throws a ReferenceError. var does not have a TDZ — it is hoisted and initialized to undefined immediately. The TDZ exists to prevent the confusing behavior of accessing a variable before it is meaningfully defined.

What is a closure? Give a real-world use case.:::A closure is a function that retains access to its outer lexical scope even after the outer function has returned. The inner function holds a reference to the variables of its enclosing environment, not copies. Real-world uses: (1) factory functions — makeCounter() returns increment/decrement functions that share private state; (2) memoization — a cache Map closed over by the returned function; (3) event handlers — the handler closes over component state; (4) module pattern — expose only a public API while keeping implementation private.

Why does this code print 3 three times: for(var i=0;i<3;i++) funcs.push(()=>i)?:::All three arrow functions close over the same binding of i — the single var i declared in function scope (not block scope). By the time any of the functions are called, the loop has finished and i === 3. Each function returns the current value of i at call time, not at definition time. Fix: replace var with let (creates a new binding per iteration), or use an IIFE to capture i's current value in a new scope.

What is the difference between a function declaration and a function expression with respect to hoisting?:::A function declaration (function foo() {}) is fully hoisted — both the name and the body are available from the top of the scope, so you can call it before its line in the code. A function expression (const foo = function() {}) hoists only the variable binding (undefined for var, TDZ for let/const), not the function itself. Calling a function expression before its assignment throws TypeError (not a function) for var, or ReferenceError for let/const.
sessions.successCriteria: Can explain hoisting, TDZ, scope chain, and closures clearly. Can implement a closure-based module and fix the classic var loop bug.

sessions.title: Prototypes & Classes — Prototype Chain, ES6 Classes, this Keyword
sessions.paretoJustification: Prototype chain and this binding are among the most common JS interview topics. Classes are used everywhere in modern codebases.
objectives: Explain the prototype chain and how property lookup works
Implement inheritance using both constructor functions and ES6 classes
Describe the four rules of this binding with examples
Explain what the new keyword does step by step

activities.description: Implement a Shape base class with area() and perimeter() methods. Extend it with Circle and Rectangle. Use instanceof and Object.getPrototypeOf to verify the chain. Compare class-based vs Object.create-based implementation.

Create an object with a method and extract it to a variable. Call it in four ways: direct call, call(), apply(), bind(). Predict and verify the value of this in each case. Then try the same with an arrow function method.

resources.title: MDN: Inheritance and the prototype chain

reviewQuestions: How does property lookup work via the prototype chain?:::When you access a property on an object, JavaScript first checks the object's own properties. If not found, it follows the internal [[Prototype]] link to the next object and checks there. This continues up the chain until the property is found or null is reached (end of chain), at which point undefined is returned. Methods like hasOwnProperty check only own properties, while the in operator checks the entire chain.

What are the four rules of this binding and what determines which applies?:::1. Default binding: standalone function call — this is undefined (strict) or globalThis (sloppy). 2. Implicit binding: method call (obj.method()) — this is obj. 3. Explicit binding: call/apply/bind — this is the first argument. 4. new binding: constructor call — this is the newly created object. Priority order: new > explicit > implicit > default. Arrow functions do not have their own this — they inherit it lexically from the enclosing scope.

What does ES6 class syntax compile to under the hood?:::ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over prototype-based inheritance. class Foo { constructor() {} method() {} } is equivalent to: a constructor function named Foo, with method added to Foo.prototype. class Bar extends Foo compiles to setting Bar.prototype = Object.create(Foo.prototype) and Bar.__proto__ = Foo (for static inheritance). The main additions beyond syntax are: private fields (#), static class fields, and proper TDZ enforcement in constructors (super() must be called before this).

What is the difference between call, apply, and bind?:::All three explicitly set the this value for a function. call(thisArg, arg1, arg2) invokes the function immediately with arguments listed individually. apply(thisArg, [arg1, arg2]) invokes it immediately with arguments as an array — useful when args are already in an array. bind(thisArg, arg1) returns a NEW function permanently bound to thisArg, not yet called — useful for callbacks and event handlers where you need to preserve this.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement class-based inheritance, explain the prototype chain, and correctly predict this in any calling context.

sessions.title: Async JavaScript — Callbacks, Promises, async/await, Event Loop
sessions.paretoJustification: Async JS is tested in nearly every frontend/fullstack interview. The event loop question appears constantly at senior levels.
objectives: Trace the execution order of sync code, microtasks, and macrotasks
Convert callback-based code to Promises and then to async/await
Use Promise.all, allSettled, race, and any correctly
Handle errors properly in async/await with try/catch and re-throwing

activities.description: Predict the output of 5 different code snippets mixing setTimeout, Promise.resolve(), queueMicrotask, and nested Promises. Run them to verify. Write explanations for each.
Convert a callback-based readFile/processData/writeFile pipeline to Promises, then to async/await. Add timeout using AbortController. Add retry with exponential backoff.

resources.title: Jake Archibald: Tasks, Microtasks, Queues and Schedules

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between a microtask and a macrotask? Give examples of each.:::Macrotasks (tasks) are scheduled by: setTimeout, setInterval, setImmediate, I/O callbacks, UI rendering, and script loading. Only ONE macrotask runs per event loop iteration. Microtasks are scheduled by: Promise.then/catch/finally, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver, and async/await (which desugars to Promise chains). After each macrotask, the engine drains the ENTIRE microtask queue before picking the next macrotask. This means nested microtasks (a .then inside a .then) all run before any setTimeout callback.

What does Promise.allSettled do differently from Promise.all?:::Promise.all rejects as soon as ANY promise in the array rejects — you get one rejection reason and the other promises' results are lost (though they still run). Promise.allSettled waits for ALL promises to complete regardless of outcome, and returns an array of result objects: { status: 'fulfilled', value } or { status: 'rejected', reason }. Use allSettled when you need to handle each result independently and cannot afford to lose any results due to one failure.

Why does await in a for loop execute sequentially rather than in parallel?:::Each await suspends the async function and waits for the promise to settle before continuing to the next iteration. So await fetchItem(ids[0]) must resolve before the loop body runs again to fetch ids[1]. To run in parallel, collect all promises first with map(), then await Promise.all(): const results = await Promise.all(ids.map(id => fetchItem(id))). This starts all fetches simultaneously and waits for all to complete.

What happens if you forget to catch a rejected promise?:::In browsers and Node.js, an unhandled promise rejection triggers an unhandledRejection event (Node) or an error in the console (browser). In Node.js, modern versions (15+) will crash the process by default on unhandled rejections. Best practices: always add .catch() or wrap await in try/catch; use a global unhandledRejection handler as a last resort; never use floating promises (promises not returned or awaited).
sessions.successCriteria: Can correctly predict event loop output, convert callback code to async/await, use Promise combinators appropriately, and implement proper error handling.

sessions.title: TypeScript Essentials — Types, Interfaces, Generics, Enums, Utility Types
sessions.paretoJustification: TypeScript is now required for most frontend/fullstack roles. Generics and utility types are the most-asked TS topics in interviews.
objectives: Distinguish type aliases from interfaces and know when to use each
Write generic functions and classes with constraints using extends keyof
Use the common utility types: Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record, ReturnType
Implement type narrowing using discriminated unions and type guards

activities.description: Build a type-safe event emitter in TypeScript using generics. The EventEmitter<EventMap> class should infer the correct callback signature for each event name. Test with at least 3 event types.

Given a plain JavaScript API client, add full TypeScript types. Use Partial for request options, union types for response status, and ReturnType/Awaited for derived types. Enable strict mode and fix all errors.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between type and interface in TypeScript? When would you choose one over the other?:::Both define shapes for objects, but they differ in capability: interfaces support declaration merging (you can re-declare the same interface to add properties — useful for extending library types), while type aliases do not. Type aliases support unions, intersections, tuple types, conditional types, and mapped types — things interfaces cannot express. Convention: use interface for public API shapes and class contracts; use type for unions, tuples, utility compositions, and anything that isn't an object literal type.

Explain generics with a real example. What problem do they solve?:::Generics allow you to write code that works over many types while maintaining type safety — the return type and parameter types are inferred from what you pass in. Without generics, you'd either use any (losing type safety) or write duplicate functions for each type. Example: function identity<T>(arg: T): T { return arg; } — TypeScript infers T from the argument, so identity('hello') returns string, and identity(42) returns number. A real case: a Stack<T> class that enforces push and pop work with the same type.

What does keyof do and how is it used in practice?:::keyof T produces a union type of all the keys of type T. Given interface User { id: string; name: string; age: number }, keyof User is 'id' | 'name' | 'age'. Combined with generics: function getProperty<T, K extends keyof T>(obj: T, key: K): T[K] — this ensures key is a valid property of obj, and the return type is exactly the type of that property. This pattern eliminates a whole class of runtime errors from typos in property names.

What is a discriminated union and why is it useful?:::A discriminated union is a union type where each member has a common literal property (the discriminant) that narrows the type. Example: type Shape = { kind: 'circle'; radius: number } | { kind: 'rect'; width: number; height: number }. When you check shape.kind === 'circle', TypeScript narrows the type to the circle branch, giving you access to radius and eliminating width/height. This pattern is safer than optional properties because TypeScript enforces that you handle all cases — especially useful with exhaustive switch statements.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write fully type-safe TypeScript code using generics, utility types, discriminated unions, and type guards without resorting to any.

sessions.title: DOM & Browser APIs — Event Handling, Fetch, Storage, Web Workers
sessions.paretoJustification: DOM manipulation and browser APIs are core to frontend interviews. Event delegation and Fetch are used in virtually every web application.
objectives: Explain event bubbling and capturing, and implement event delegation
Build a robust fetch wrapper with error handling, JSON parsing, and AbortController
Compare localStorage, sessionStorage, cookies, and IndexedDB — when to use each
Use Web Workers to move CPU-intensive work off the main thread

activities.description: Build a dynamic todo list using event delegation on the container. Adding, completing, and deleting items should all use one event listener. Persist state to localStorage.
Implement a fetch wrapper with retry (3 attempts, exponential backoff), request timeout (AbortController), and response caching in a Map. Test with a slow/failing endpoint.

reviewQuestions: What is event delegation and why is it preferred over attaching listeners to each element?:::Event delegation uses bubbling to attach one listener on a parent element instead of individual listeners on each child. When a child is clicked, the event bubbles up to the parent handler, which uses event.target to determine which child was clicked. Benefits: (1) fewer listeners means less memory, (2) dynamically added children are automatically handled without re-attaching listeners, (3) simpler cleanup — remove one listener instead of many.

What is the difference between localStorage and sessionStorage?:::Both are synchronous key-value stores accessible via the Web Storage API, storing data as strings. localStorage persists until explicitly cleared — it survives tab closes, browser restarts, and is shared across all tabs of the same origin. sessionStorage is scoped to a single tab and is cleared when that tab is closed. Data in sessionStorage is NOT shared between tabs even on the same origin. Use localStorage for persistent user preferences; sessionStorage for temporary per-tab state like multi-step form drafts.

What is the purpose of AbortController with fetch?:::AbortController provides a way to cancel in-flight fetch requests. You create a controller, pass its signal to fetch, and call controller.abort() to cancel. The fetch promise rejects with an AbortError. Common use cases: (1) request timeout — abort after N milliseconds; (2) component unmount — cancel in-flight requests when a React component unmounts to prevent state updates on dead components; (3) user-cancelled operations — search-as-you-type where old requests should be dropped.

Why can't Web Workers access the DOM? What can they access?:::The DOM is not thread-safe. Allowing multiple threads to read and write DOM nodes simultaneously would require complex locking or produce race conditions. Web Workers run in a completely separate thread with no shared memory to the main thread (communication is via postMessage with structured cloning). Workers can access: fetch, XMLHttpRequest, WebSockets, IndexedDB, setTimeout/setInterval, importScripts, Math, JSON, and typed arrays. They cannot access: document, window, parent DOM, localStorage (in some implementations), or any UI APIs.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement event delegation, a robust fetch wrapper, correct storage strategy selection, and off-thread computation with Web Workers.

sessions.title: ES6+ Features — Destructuring, Spread, Map/Set, Symbol, Proxy
sessions.paretoJustification: Modern JS syntax is used in every codebase. Symbol and Proxy appear in senior-level interviews testing deep language knowledge.
objectives: Use destructuring with nested objects, defaults, and renaming
Distinguish Map from plain objects and Set from arrays — complexity and use cases
Explain Symbol and its role in creating non-enumerable unique keys
Implement a Proxy with get, set, and has traps for validation or reactive behavior

activities.description: Implement a reactive data store using Proxy that: tracks which properties have been accessed (get trap), validates values against a schema (set trap), and notifies subscribers on change (set trap). Test with a user object.
Solve 3 problems using Map and Set: (1) count word frequency in a string, (2) find the first duplicate in an array, (3) compute union, intersection, and difference of two arrays of numbers.

reviewQuestions: When should you use a Map instead of a plain object?:::Use Map when: (1) keys are not strings/symbols — Map accepts any value as a key including objects, functions, and numbers; (2) you need insertion-order iteration guaranteed; (3) you need O(1) size via .size (object requires Object.keys(obj).length); (4) keys are dynamically generated and might conflict with Object prototype properties (e.g., 'constructor', 'toString'). Use plain objects for: static, string-keyed configurations and when JSON serialization is needed (Map does not serialize to JSON by default).

What is the purpose of Symbol and why are Symbol properties not enumerable?:::Symbol creates globally unique primitive values — even Symbol('id') === Symbol('id') is false. They are used as non-colliding property keys, especially in libraries and built-in protocols. Symbol properties are excluded from Object.keys(), JSON.stringify(), and for...in loops — they're only accessible via obj[symbol] or Object.getOwnPropertySymbols(). This makes them ideal for metadata and extension points that shouldn't interfere with normal object enumeration or serialization.

What is optional chaining (?.) and how does it differ from a regular property access?:::Optional chaining short-circuits property access chains when it encounters null or undefined, returning undefined instead of throwing a TypeError. user?.address?.city returns undefined if user or user.address is null/undefined, rather than crashing. This is equivalent to user && user.address && user.address.city but more concise. It also works with method calls (user?.getAddress?.()) and array access (users?.[0]?.name). It does NOT help if the value is 0, false, or empty string — those are valid values.

How does Proxy differ from Object.defineProperty for interception?:::Object.defineProperty intercepts get/set for specific, pre-defined properties at the time of definition — it's per-property and synchronous. Proxy wraps an entire object and intercepts ALL operations including: property access (get), assignment (set), property existence checks (has), function calls (apply), constructor calls (construct), property deletion (deleteProperty), and more. Proxy handles dynamic properties that don't exist yet, which defineProperty cannot. Reflect is commonly paired with Proxy to forward operations to the original target.
sessions.successCriteria: Can use all destructuring forms fluently, choose Map/Set over arrays/objects when appropriate, and implement a Proxy-based validation or observation pattern.

sessions.title: Error Handling & Debugging — try/catch, Error Types, DevTools
sessions.paretoJustification: Robust error handling separates production-quality code from hobby projects. DevTools profiling is tested in performance-focused interviews.
objectives: Identify the built-in JavaScript error types and when each is thrown
Implement a custom error hierarchy with meaningful metadata
Apply correct try/catch patterns including re-throwing and finally
Use Chrome DevTools to debug, profile, and identify memory issues

activities.description: Build a fetchWithRetry function that retries on NetworkError but NOT on ValidationError. Use instanceof to distinguish error types. Implement exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s) with a max of 3 retries.
Use the Performance panel in Chrome DevTools to record a page interaction. Identify the longest task. Use the Memory panel to take two heap snapshots and find objects retained between them.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between throw, re-throw, and wrapping an error?:::throw creates and raises a new error, starting a new error chain. Re-throw (catch(e) { throw e; }) propagates the original error unchanged — the original stack trace is preserved. Wrapping creates a new error that includes the original as a cause: throw new AppError('context', { cause: originalError }). Re-throw when you cannot handle the error at this level. Wrap when you need to add context (HTTP status, error code) but preserve the original for debugging. Never swallow errors silently in catch blocks.

When does the finally block run? Can it suppress an error?:::finally always runs after the try block completes, regardless of whether it succeeded or threw. It also runs after a return or throw in the catch block. Importantly, if finally contains a return statement, it OVERRIDES any return value or thrown error from try/catch — the error is silently discarded. This is a common footgun. Never use return inside finally unless you intentionally want to suppress errors.

What is the Error.cause property and when should you use it?:::Error.cause (ES2022) lets you pass a causing error to a new Error constructor: new Error('high level message', { cause: originalError }). This creates an error chain that preserves the original stack trace for debugging while adding contextual information. Use it when wrapping low-level errors (network, database) with domain-specific errors (PaymentError, UserNotFoundError). The cause is available as error.cause and many logging libraries now display the full error chain.

Explain the difference between a TypeError and a ReferenceError.:::TypeError is thrown when an operation is applied to a value of the wrong type: calling null as a function, accessing a property on null/undefined, calling a non-function. ReferenceError is thrown when accessing a variable that has not been declared in any scope, or accessing a let/const during its Temporal Dead Zone. In production, TypeErrors are by far the most common — they indicate null/undefined propagating where an object was expected. ReferenceErrors usually indicate a typo or missing import.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement a custom error hierarchy, apply correct try/catch/finally patterns, and use DevTools to debug and profile real issues.

sessions.title: Modules & Bundling — ESM, CommonJS, Webpack/Vite Basics
sessions.paretoJustification: Module systems underpin every modern JS project. Understanding the difference between ESM and CJS is essential for Node.js and frontend roles.
objectives: Write CommonJS and ESM modules and explain the syntax differences
Explain static vs dynamic imports and when to use each
Describe how tree shaking works and why it requires ESM
Understand the role of bundlers and the difference between Webpack and Vite

activities.description: Create a small utility library in both CommonJS and ESM format. Import it both ways. Use dynamic import() for lazy loading. Verify tree shaking by comparing bundle sizes with and without unused exports.
Set up a minimal Vite project from scratch. Add TypeScript. Configure path aliases (@/ for src/). Build for production and analyze the bundle with rollup-plugin-visualizer.

reviewQuestions: Why does tree shaking require ESM and not CommonJS?:::Tree shaking statically analyzes which exports are imported and eliminates unused ones from the bundle. This is only possible with ESM because imports and exports are statically declared — the bundler knows at parse time exactly what is imported and from where. CommonJS uses require() which is a runtime function call that can be inside if statements, loops, or dynamic expressions — the bundler cannot safely determine which exports are actually used without executing the code.

What is the difference between static import and dynamic import()?:::Static imports (import x from 'module') are hoisted and resolved before any code runs. They create a live binding to the exported value and enable tree shaking. Dynamic imports (import('module')) return a Promise and load the module lazily — only when that line of code executes. Use dynamic import for code splitting (loading heavy modules on demand), conditional loading (polyfills), and plugin systems where the module name is determined at runtime.

Why does CommonJS use synchronous require() but ESM uses asynchronous loading?:::CommonJS was designed for Node.js where modules are on the local filesystem — synchronous reads are fast and don't block meaningfully. ESM was designed for the web where modules may be fetched over the network. Synchronous network fetches would freeze the browser. ESM uses a three-phase process: construction (parse, fetch), instantiation (create bindings), evaluation (execute) — all asynchronous. This also enables top-level await in ESM, which would be impossible with synchronous loading.

What is content hashing in bundler output filenames and why does it matter?:::Content hashing appends a hash derived from the file's content to the filename (e.g., main.a1b2c3d4.js). When the file content changes, the hash changes, giving a new URL — forcing browsers to download the new version. When content is unchanged, the hash is the same, and browsers serve the cached version. This enables aggressive cache headers (cache-control: immutable, max-age=31536000) while still ensuring users always get the latest code after a deployment.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write ESM and CJS modules, explain tree shaking, use dynamic imports for code splitting, and configure a basic Vite or Webpack project.

sessions.title: Testing — Jest, Testing Library, Mocking, TDD
sessions.paretoJustification: Testing knowledge is increasingly expected at mid-to-senior levels. Jest and Testing Library are the dominant tools in JS/TS codebases.
objectives: Write unit tests with Jest using describe, test, expect, and test.each
Mock modules, functions, and fetch responses
Use Testing Library to test React components from the user's perspective
Apply TDD: Red-Green-Refactor cycle to implement a feature

activities.description: Using TDD, implement a validatePassword function that requires: min 8 chars, one uppercase, one number, one special char. Write the tests first (at least 8 cases — valid and each invalid condition), then implement.

Write integration tests for a UserService class that uses an injected HTTP client. Mock the HTTP client to return controlled data. Test the happy path and at least two error cases (404, network error).

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between jest.fn(), jest.mock(), and jest.spyOn()?:::jest.fn() creates a standalone mock function with no implementation — you define behavior via .mockReturnValue(), .mockResolvedValue(), etc. jest.mock('module') replaces an entire module with auto-mocked or factory-defined versions — affects all imports of that module in the test file. jest.spyOn(object, 'method') wraps an existing method with a spy while preserving (or optionally replacing) the original implementation — useful for verifying calls to real objects. Always restore spies with .mockRestore() in afterEach.

Why does Testing Library discourage using CSS selectors and component internals in tests?:::Testing Library's philosophy is: test what users see and do, not implementation details. CSS selectors like .btn-primary test CSS class names, which can change without breaking functionality. Component props and state test internals invisible to users. If you refactor the implementation but behavior is unchanged, selector-based tests break unnecessarily (false positives). User-centric queries (getByRole, getByLabelText, getByText) only break when behavior visible to the user changes — which is when you WANT tests to fail.

What is test.each and when is it useful?:::test.each runs the same test function multiple times with different inputs from a data table. It's useful for parameterized tests where the assertion logic is identical but you want to cover many input combinations: edge cases, boundary values, and equivalent partitions. Without test.each, you'd either duplicate test code (brittle) or write one test with a loop (loses individual test names and isolation). test.each generates a named test case for each row, so failures are clearly identified by their input values.

Describe the difference between a unit test, integration test, and end-to-end test.:::Unit tests test a single function or class in isolation, with all dependencies mocked. Fast, granular, catch regressions early. Integration tests test multiple units working together — e.g., a service class using a real (or in-memory) database, or a component tree with real child components but mocked API calls. End-to-end tests run the full application in a real or headless browser (Cypress, Playwright) from the user's perspective — no mocks, real API calls. The testing pyramid: many unit tests, some integration tests, few E2E tests.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write comprehensive unit and integration tests with Jest, mock dependencies correctly, use Testing Library with user-centric queries, and follow TDD.

sessions.title: Performance & Memory — Memory Leaks, Debounce/Throttle, Web Vitals
sessions.paretoJustification: Performance optimization questions appear in senior interviews. Debounce/throttle implementations are classic coding questions. Memory leaks are a common production issue.
objectives: Identify the four most common JavaScript memory leak patterns
Implement debounce and throttle from scratch without libraries
Explain the Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, INP) and how to measure them
Use requestIdleCallback and Web Workers to keep the main thread responsive

activities.description: Implement debounce and throttle from scratch with full TypeScript types. Include leading/trailing edge options. Write unit tests using jest.useFakeTimers() to control time. Test edge cases: rapid calls, leading edge, trailing edge.
Use Chrome DevTools Memory panel to find a memory leak in a provided component that adds event listeners without removing them. Fix the leak and verify with before/after heap snapshots.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between debounce and throttle? When would you use each?:::Debounce delays execution until N milliseconds have passed since the LAST call — it resets the timer on every call. Use it when you only care about the final state after activity stops: search input (wait for typing to pause), window resize (update layout after resizing ends), form validation (validate after user finishes typing). Throttle ensures a function runs at most once per N milliseconds regardless of call rate. Use it when you want regular updates during continuous activity: scroll position tracking, mousemove, progress reporting, rate-limiting API calls.

Describe four common JavaScript memory leak patterns.:::1. Event listeners not removed — attaching listeners in setup without corresponding removeEventListener in teardown; listeners hold references to outer scope objects. 2. Closures over large objects — an inner function returned and kept alive closes over a large outer variable even when only a small part is needed. 3. Detached DOM nodes — elements removed from the DOM but still referenced in JS arrays or Maps, preventing GC. 4. setInterval/setTimeout without clearing — interval callbacks keep running and hold closure references even after the triggering component is destroyed.

What are the Core Web Vitals and what does each measure?:::LCP (Largest Contentful Paint): when the largest image or text block becomes visible — measures loading performance. Target: under 2.5 seconds. CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift): sum of unexpected layout shifts — measures visual stability. Target: under 0.1. INP (Interaction to Next Paint, replaced FID in 2024): time from user interaction to next visual update — measures responsiveness. Target: under 200ms. TTFB (Time to First Byte) and FCP (First Contentful Paint) are supporting metrics, not Core Web Vitals, but inform LCP optimization.

How does using WeakMap or WeakRef help prevent memory leaks?:::WeakMap holds keys weakly — if the key object has no other strong references, it can be garbage collected, and the WeakMap entry is automatically removed. This prevents the WeakMap from keeping objects alive longer than needed. WeakRef holds a weak reference to an object — the object can be GC'd independently, and weakRef.deref() returns undefined if it has been collected. Use WeakMap to associate metadata with DOM nodes or objects without preventing their collection. Use WeakRef in caches where entries should be dropped under memory pressure.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement debounce and throttle from scratch, identify and fix memory leaks, explain Core Web Vitals, and apply techniques to keep the main thread responsive.

quizBank.question:
What will the following code log?
```js
console.log(typeof null);
```
quizBank.explanation: typeof null returns 'object' — this is a well-known JavaScript bug that has been preserved for backwards compatibility since JS 1.0. null is a primitive value, but typeof reports it as 'object'. Always use === null to check for null, never typeof.

quizBank.question: What is the output of: `console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3)`?
quizBank.explanation: JavaScript uses IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic. 0.1 + 0.2 evaluates to 0.30000000000000004, not exactly 0.3. Use Math.abs(a - b) < Number.EPSILON for floating-point comparisons, or multiply to integers before comparing.

quizBank.question: Which of the following creates a SHALLOW copy of an object?
quizBank.explanation: Object.assign({}, obj) and spread ({ ...obj }) create shallow copies — nested objects are still referenced, not copied. JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(obj)) and structuredClone() both create deep copies. Object.freeze() does not copy — it makes the original immutable.

quizBank.question: What does the `in` operator check for in JavaScript?
options: Only own properties of an object
A property anywhere in the prototype chain
Whether a value exists in an array
Whether a key exists in a Map
quizBank.correctAnswer: A property anywhere in the prototype chain
quizBank.explanation: The `in` operator checks for property existence anywhere in the prototype chain, including inherited properties. 'toString' in {} returns true because toString is on Object.prototype. Use hasOwnProperty() or Object.hasOwn() to check only own properties.

quizBank.question: In TypeScript, what does the `never` type represent?
options: A value that can never occur — e.g., a function that always throws
quizBank.correctAnswer: A value that can never occur — e.g., a function that always throws
quizBank.explanation: never represents values that never occur. Use cases: (1) function that always throws or infinitely loops (return type never), (2) exhaustiveness checking in switch statements — if all union cases are handled, the default branch should be assignable to never. never is a subtype of every type but no type is a subtype of never.

Execution order: (1) Synchronous code runs first: A, D. (2) Microtask queue drains: C (Promise.resolve().then is a microtask). (3) Macrotask queue: B (setTimeout, even with 0ms delay, is a macrotask). The microtask queue is always fully drained before the next macrotask.

quizBank.question: What does `Array.prototype.reduce()` return when called on an empty array without an initial value?
quizBank.explanation: Calling reduce() on an empty array without an initial value throws TypeError: Reduce of empty array with no initial value. Always provide an initial value when the input array might be empty: arr.reduce((acc, val) => ..., initialValue).

quizBank.question: What is the difference between `==` and `===` in JavaScript?
quizBank.correctAnswer: === checks value AND type without coercion; == checks value after type coercion
quizBank.explanation: === (strict equality) returns true only if both value and type are identical — no type conversion. == (loose equality) converts operands to the same type before comparing (type coercion). Examples: 0 == false is true, 0 === false is false. '1' == 1 is true, '1' === 1 is false. Always prefer === in production code to avoid subtle coercion bugs.

quizBank.question: Which TypeScript utility type makes all properties of T optional?
quizBank.explanation: Partial<T> constructs a type with all properties of T set to optional. It is equivalent to { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] }. Required<T> does the opposite. Readonly<T> makes all properties readonly. Pick<T, K> selects a subset of properties.

quizBank.question: What is a WeakMap and when should you use it?
quizBank.correctAnswer: A Map with weakly held object keys — entries are GC'd when the key object has no other references
quizBank.explanation: WeakMap holds its keys weakly — if the key object becomes unreachable (no other strong references), the garbage collector can collect it and the WeakMap entry disappears automatically. Keys must be objects. WeakMap has no iteration methods (no .keys(), .values(), .entries(), no .size). Use cases: associating private data with DOM elements, caching computed results keyed by objects, storing metadata without preventing GC of the associated objects.
